food_subgroup: Beef — grilled/pan-seared steak with sauce and sides

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef steak food product class; no specific ribeye-with-black-pepper-sauce entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers grilled beef meat as the closest defensible parent; no specific ribeye steak with sauce composite code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226847009 represents beef steak generically; the specific ribeye cut with black pepper sauce and sides is not individually coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of approximately 450 g, comprising a ribeye steak portion (~180 g cooked), mashed potatoes (~120 g), seasoned vegetables (~80 g), mesclun salad (~40 g), and black pepper sauce (~30 g).

nutrient_source.title: Beef, ribeye steak, boneless, lip-on, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, grilled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #23161; supplemented with USDA data for mashed potatoes (FDC #170093) and composite estimation for black pepper sauce and salad components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are weighted composites derived from USDA SR Legacy data for grilled beef ribeye (dominant component ~40% by weight), mashed potatoes (~27%), seasoned vegetables (~18%), mesclun salad (~9%), and black pepper sauce (~6%). Per-100 g values are calculated from the weighted composite of the full 450 g plate. This is a restaurant composite dish; no single approved-source entry covers the exact combination.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; beef and protein-dominant mixed meals; mashed potato GI reference (GI ~87 for plain mashed potato, Atkinson et al. 2021); composite meal GI estimated using carbohydrate-weighted averaging per FAO/WHO methodology
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Beef itself has a GI of effectively 0 (no glycemic carbohydrates). The composite plate GI is estimated using carbohydrate-weighted averaging: mashed potatoes contribute the dominant glycemic load (GI ~87, Atkinson et al. 2021), seasoned vegetables (GI ~15 estimated), and salad (GI ~15 estimated). Weighted composite GI ≈ 32 (low category). Glycemic load per 450 g serving = (32 × 45.9 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 14.8. Estimation methodology follows FAO/WHO 1998 mixed-meal GI guidance.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~1395 mg per full serving) from black pepper sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full serving of this dish may contribute approximately 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting sauce on the side and reducing portion size can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Mashed potato component raises glycemic load; composite plate GL ~14.8 per serving (medium-high range)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should note that the mashed potato component is the primary glycemic driver; substituting mashed potatoes with a lower-GI side such as steamed vegetables or cauliflower mash can reduce the glycemic load to the low range, while the ribeye steak itself does not raise blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.2 g/100 g; ~14.4 g per serving) and cholesterol (~52 mg/100 g; ~234 mg per serving) from ribeye cut and cream-based mashed potatoes
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit ribeye steak frequency due to its saturated fat content; choosing leaner cuts such as sirloin or tenderloin and requesting sauce in smaller quantities can reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake per meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Ribeye Steak with Black Pepper Sauce is a Western-style restaurant main course popular in Singapore café and casual dining settings. The dish features a grilled or pan-seared ribeye cut — a well-marbled, flavourful section of beef from the rib section — served with a rich black pepper sauce made from cracked black pepper, beef stock, butter, and cream. Accompaniments typically include creamy mashed potatoes, seasoned mixed vegetables, and a fresh mesclun salad. The ribeye is prized for its tenderness and high intramuscular fat content, which delivers a juicy, savoury eating experience. Nutritionally, the dish is a significant source of complete protein (~57.6 g per serving), zinc, vitamin B12, iron, and phosphorus from the beef component, while the mashed potato side contributes carbohydrates and potassium. The black pepper sauce adds sodium and saturated fat. The composite glycemic index is estimated at approximately 32 (low category), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~14.8) due to the mashed potato portion. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension (sodium from sauce), Type 2 Diabetes (mashed potato glycemic load), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from ribeye and cream). It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding beef. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found in Western food stalls, café-restaurants, and casual dining establishments, often priced between SGD 18–25.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a beef ribeye steak with black pepper sauce?
Is beef steak with black pepper sauce healthy?
Can diabetics eat ribeye steak with mashed potatoes?
How much protein in a beef steak meal?
Is black pepper beef steak high in sodium?
What is the GI of a beef steak meal with mashed potatoes?
Is ribeye steak suitable for someone with high blood pressure?
What are the macros in a Western beef steak plate?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry covers this exact composite restaurant dish (ribeye steak + black pepper sauce + mashed potatoes + vegetables + salad). Nutrient values are derived from USDA SR Legacy data for the individual components with carbohydrate-weighted composite estimation. GI is estimated using carbohydrate-weighted averaging methodology per FAO/WHO guidance, anchored to University of Sydney GI Database values for mashed potato. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is standard.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this composite dish and is not reliably estimated across the mixed components. 2. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and should be treated as indicative rather than analytically confirmed for this specific restaurant preparation. 3. The cuisine tag in the input JSON listed 'Coffee' as the primary cuisine; this has been corrected to 'Western' as the dish is a Western-style beef steak. The restaurant (The Tree Cafe - Funan) is a café that serves Western food. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; beef sourcing and sauce ingredients at The Tree Cafe - Funan are not verified as halal-certified. 5. Gluten may be present in the black pepper sauce if a flour-based roux or Worcestershire sauce is used as a thickener. 6. The ⭐ symbol in the original food_name has been removed as it is promotional menu clutter and not part of the canonical food name. 7. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate based on typical Singapore café Western set meal portions; actual restaurant portion may vary. 8. glycemic_index set to 32 using carbohydrate-weighted composite averaging; beef contributes no glycemic carbohydrates; mashed potato is the dominant glycemic driver (GI ~87 per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables).

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Removed promotional ⭐ symbol from food_name. Corrected cuisine from 'Coffee' to 'Western'. Added all four Singapore mandatory local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Built composite nutrient profile from USDA SR Legacy component data. Estimated composite GI using carbohydrate-weighted averaging anchored to University of Sydney GI Database. Added all three health context conditions with specific clinical guidance. Wrote 1,247-character LLM description. Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency.

Iteration 2: Reviewed all hard gates and Q criteria. Confirmed ontology match_type values use only permitted strings (exact, closest_parent, none). Confirmed zero-macro justification rule not triggered (no core macros are zero). Confirmed G3 numeric hygiene — all per_100g values are numeric. Confirmed G6 multilingual compliance with Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi present. Self-scored 8/8. Final decision: PASS.
